biases.name: Density axis
biases.rule: prefer 1-2 attacks per beat; reserve 3-4 attacks for arrivals (phrase ends, cadences, climaxes)

biases.name: Vertical spread axis
biases.rule: prefer 3-5 octave spread; avoid SATB-like 2-octave middle-register cluster
biases.penalty_target: narrow spread in middle register for sustained passages

biases.name: SATB-zone avoidance
biases.rule: penalize combinations with high density + narrow spread + middle register + on-the-beat block attacks

biases.name: Expose the extremes
biases.rule: the audience tracks the highest and the lowest voice — keep both clear. Prefer voicings where the top (melody) and the bottom (bass) are registrally isolated from the middle; put color in the middle instead of close-position chord tones directly under the melody
biases.penalty_target: (a) melody + plus_1/plus_2 cluster just below the top; (b) bass crowded into mid-register fill rather than sitting low; (c) no LH strike below string 15 (C3) for the entire hymn

dimensions.name: Shape selection under the melody note
dimensions.note: WHICH chord sits under the melody. The 118-fraction pool is a vocabulary of harp-specific chord shapes: 42 'path' fractions that walk the six trefoil cycles (2nds/3rds/4ths × CW/CCW), and 76 'reserve' fractions held for color and substitution. The melody note is always the top voice — tactics in this dimension pick whether the shape below it is a full 4-finger pool chord, a quartal stack, a triad, a sparse dyad, or nothing at all. Strictly diatonic: no tritone subs, no modal interchange, no chromatic alterations.

dimensions.name: Register placement
dimensions.note: Which octave(s) the chord lives in. A pedal harp has 47 strings spanning 7 octaves; the same shape played low vs. high has a very different color — low is boomy and fundamental-heavy, high is bright and partial-heavy, middle-register stacking produces the 'muddy' sound. Tactics here shift the shape up or down relative to the melody note, or spread it across two octaves. Related to but distinct from 'range' (which is about the whole piece).

dimensions.name: Non-melody hand activity
dimensions.note: What the LH actually does beat-by-beat. On a harp, strings ring after plucking, so the LH doesn't need to restrike to keep a chord sounding. 'Strike and ring' is the harp-idiomatic default (one LH attack on beat 1, no restrike); 'partial silence' is jazz-style sparse comping (e.g. hits on 2-and + 4); 'low_bass_grab' is a grace-note dive to sub-bass before the main attack; 'none' is melody-alone in the RH.

dimensions.name: Melody hand activity beyond the melody note
dimensions.note: What the RH adds around the melody. Doubling the melody an octave up, double-stops (two-note chord with the melody on top), inner-voice fills, broken-chord figuration under the melody, running 16ths, or nothing beyond the melody note itself. More RH activity = more perceived density; too much competes with the LH.

dimensions.name: Connection from previous measure
dimensions.note: How the current measure's opening relates to the previous measure's ending. Because the harp articulates every note (plucked, not bowed), perceived continuity across bar lines comes from voicing choice, not legato phrasing. Tactics include common-tone pivot (a pitch from the previous chord held into this one), approach-from-larger/smaller interval, release (let the previous ring out without competition), and chromatic neighbor approach.

dimensions.name: Connection to next measure
dimensions.note: Mirror of connect_from — how this measure's ending sets up the next. The same vocabulary applies (common tones, approach tones, release, larger/smaller intervals), but on the outgoing side of the bar line. A strong connect_to + a strong connect_from on the next bar = smooth phrase; both missing = each bar feels isolated.

dimensions.name: Harmonic substitution at measure scale
dimensions.note: Replacing the expected diatonic chord with a related one. In this strictly-diatonic system the legal subs are: quality swap (V ↔ V7, I ↔ IΔ), deceptive (V → vi, V → VI in minor), third-substitution (ii → IV, the chord a third above), and common-tone pivot (swap to any chord that shares ≥2 pitches). NOT legal: tritone sub, ♭II7, altered dominants, harmonic-minor V (handled separately by four fallback strategies). Every sub must keep the melody note playable in the new shape.

dimensions.name: Density
dimensions.note: How many notes are sounding at once. Sparse (1–2 notes), medium (3–4), dense (5+). Harp resonance rewards sparseness: because strings sustain, fewer simultaneous notes lets each be heard; stacking too many produces harmonic mud. Density interacts with register — a dense chord low is muddier than the same chord high.

dimensions.name: Texture
dimensions.note: The sonic character of the measure independent of which notes are played. Block chord (all notes attacked together), rolled/arpeggiated (staggered low-to-high, the harp-idiomatic default), staggered (rolled more slowly, a conscious ornament), bisbigliando (whispered tremolo on adjacent strings in unison tuning), single-line (melody alone, no harmony). Texture choice shapes perceived rhythm.

dimensions.name: Lever state
dimensions.note: Lever / pedal harp configuration — which strings are flipped to accidentals. On a lever harp, each lever raises one string a semitone; on a pedal harp, each pedal raises (or lowers) every string of that letter. Changes cost a physical gesture (and time). Tactics here: don't flip (stay in the starting key), flip-and-back (brief accidental then restore), flip-hold (a modulation-like shift that persists), use-flipped (exploit an already-engaged lever), prepare-next (change a lever now for a use one bar later).

dimensions.name: Range exploitation
dimensions.note: How widely the arrangement spans the harp's range across the whole piece. 'Stay' (narrow band, typically middle register), migrate_up / migrate_down (gradual drift over several bars), split (LH low + RH high with a deliberate gap between), extreme (uses the lowest sub-bass and highest top octaves). Distinct from 'register' — register is a per-measure octave choice; range is about the trajectory.

dimensions.name: Measure role in phrase
dimensions.note: The function of this measure within its phrase. Opening (phrase first bar, establishes the harmony), middle (sustains momentum, variety), cadence_approach (penultimate, builds tension — typically more density/dissonance), cadence (the resolution), release (post-cadence settle, typically sparse). Different tactics are appropriate at different phrase positions; e.g. a dense RH fill fits cadence_approach but clashes on a release bar.

tactics.name: Drop bass to sub-bass, keep rest
Wide spread: bass low, melody high, empty middle
Compressed: both hands within two octaves
Sustain the shape (one attack, let ring)
No LH activity (because no LH shape)
tactics.note: label tactic — selector picks this exactly when shape.no_lh is chosen, so the lh_activity dimension always has a legal pick

tactics.name: Partial silence — LH strikes on some beats, rests on others
tactics.note: classic sparse-comping pattern: LH hits e.g. beat 2-and + beat 4-and, rests on downbeats while the ring carries

tactics.name: Single strike at bar start, let harp ring
tactics.note: harp-idiomatic default: one LH attack on beat 1, no restrikes; the long-sustain of harp strings fills the measure

tactics.name: Low-bass grace note before LH shape strum
tactics.note: grace-note dive to a very low bass (e.g. sub-bass octave) played just before the LH chord strum; an ornament that anchors the bottom of the texture

tactics.name: Melody anticipating next measure's chord tone
Melody held, inner finger adds passing tone
Shape released, new shape struck fresh
Land on target shape by downbeat
Anticipate target shape on last beat
Delay arrival to beat 2 of next measure
Use the written chord's library shape
Diatonic substitute (vi for I, iii for I, IV for ii)
Pedal point: hold previous chord, let melody imply new harmony
Sus version of the written chord
Single-line only
tactics.note: One melodic line, no harmony beneath. melody_alone and melody_oct are both compatible (octave-doubling still reads as a single line). Close-position doubling (melody_plus_1, melody_plus_2) is not — it becomes the 'kid banging' cluster the teacher warned about.

tactics.name: One flip at start, flip back at end
One flip at start, stay flipped
Split: one hand stays, one moves
